topic: Religion, Philosophy & Ethics: Dialogue Between Religious & Non-Religious Beliefs
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What does the Abrahamic covenant in Judaism involve?
items.correct: God's promise to Abraham of land, descendants and blessing, in return for faithfulness — the foundational covenant marking Jews as a covenant people with responsibilities, not privileges.
items.misconception: Being 'chosen' means Jews believe they are superior to other peoples.
items.random_wrong: The covenant means Jews must not interact with non-Jews.
items.why: Chosenness in Jewish theology means chosen for responsibility (following Torah, being a 'light to the nations'), not chosen for favour. Students misread it as ethnic supremacy, missing the obligation it imposes.

items.q: What does Buddhism teach about suffering?
items.correct: All existence involves dukkha (suffering/unsatisfactoriness), caused by tanha (craving and attachment); ending craving leads to liberation from suffering.
items.misconception: Buddhism teaches that life is nothing but pain and misery.
items.random_wrong: Buddhism teaches that suffering is caused by demons.
items.why: Dukkha is broader than pain — it includes dissatisfaction and impermanence. Buddhism doesn't say life is misery; it says even pleasure carries inherent unsatisfactoriness because it ends.

items.q: What is the Eightfold Path in Buddhism?
items.correct: The practical path to ending suffering: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration — grouped into wisdom, ethical conduct and mental discipline.
items.misconception: The Eightfold Path is the same as the Four Noble Truths.
items.random_wrong: The Eightfold Path is eight gods Buddhists must please.
items.why: The Four Noble Truths diagnose suffering; the Eightfold Path is the fourth truth — the prescription. They are connected but distinct. Students conflate them or treat the Path as a deity-list.
It's Rahner. “Hick” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which Vatican II document addressed other religions?
items.why: Correct answer: Nostra Aetate. “Dei Verbum” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Nostra Aetate promulgated?
items.why: Exact answer: 1965 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
